coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon quantum dots-Bi4O5Br2 composites: A case of van der Waals heterojunctions for efficient photocatalytic removal of NO under visible light irradiation

coredata.dc:description: The construction of van der Waals (VDW) heterojunctions has been deemed as an effective manner to boost photocatalytic performance since the presence of weak VDW force instead of strong chemical bonds ensures structural flexibility with diminished stress and defects caused by lattice mismatch. For such, a series of VDW heterojunctions were fabricated by decorating 2D Bi4O5Br2 with 0D N-CQDs and comprehensively characterized. Accordingly, the successful establishment of 0D-2D N-CQDs-Bi4O5Br2 (CD-BOB) VDW heterojunction composites was confirmed. These VDW heterojunctions were employed for photocatalytic removal of NO at part per billion (ppb) level and showed enhanced photocatalytic outcome compared with BOB under visible light, mainly attributing to structural merits of 0D-2D morphologies integration, promoted transfer and separation of charge carriers, boosted adsorption and activation of O2 and H2O as well as reactants NO confirmed by density functional theory (DFT) calculations, and strengthened generation of abundant of reactive species. The best candidate 5 % CD-BOB achieved a maximum removal efficiency of 51 %, which was 1.89 times higher than that of BOB alone. Besides, the robust sample was able to efficiently inhibit the formation of toxic NO2. Moreover, a preliminary photocatalysis mechanism was eventually proposed based on the analytical and experimental results. This study offers valuable insights into constructing 0D-2D VDW heterojunction composites for efficiently photocatalytically removing NO under visible light illumination.

SEM images of BOB (a) and 5 % CD-BOB (b); TEM images of BOB (c) and 5 % CD-BOB (d); HRTEM images of BOB (e) and 5 % CD-BOB (f).
The full-scan XPS spectra (a) and high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (b), N 1s (c), Bi 4f (d), Br 3d (e), and O 1s (f) for BOB and 5 % CD-BOB.

UV-Vis DRS spectra of BOB and CD-BOB series (a); side views of constructed models for BOB and CD-BOB (b); PDOS of BOB and CD-BOB (c); M-S plots and relevant band structures of BOB (d) and 5 % CD-BOB (e).

Photocatalytic NO removal (a), relevant Kapp constants (b), DeNOX index (c), removal efficiency of NO and NOX, the selectivity for NO2 and NO2 -/NO3 - species by monitoring (d), measured NO2 -/NO3 - concentrations (e) by BOB and CD-BOB series under visible light; XPS spectra of N1s in 5 % CD-BOB via soaking in NO2 -/NO3 - solution and photocatalytic NO removal after 200 min (f); fitted EIS spectra of BOB and CD-BOB series (g); Bode plots of BOB and 5 % CD-BOB (h); NO removal and NO2 generation through successive repeated experiments (i), and the long-term experiment with the cycle after washing (j) by using 5 % CD-BOB; XRD patterns (k) and XPS spectra (l) of 5 % CD-BOB before and after five successive cycles.

Calculated variation in charge density distributions for O2 and H2O adsorption on BOB and CD-BOB (a); DFT calculations of energy change along the possible removal route NO···NO∗···NO2 -···NO2···NO3 - by BOB and CD-BOB (b).
Photocatalytic NO removal by BOB (a) and 5 % CD-BOB (b) with various scavengers; ESR spectra of adducts DMPO-∙O2 - in methanol (c) and TEMP-1O2 in water (d) by 5 % CD-BOB.
The schematic photocatalysis mechanism of 0D-2D CD-BOB VDW heterojunction composites for NO removal under visible light.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The construction of van der Waals (VDW) heterojunctions has been deemed as an effective manner to boost photocatalytic performance since the presence of weak VDW force instead of strong chemical bonds ensures structural flexibility with diminished stress and defects caused by lattice mismatch. For such, a series of VDW heterojunctions were fabricated by decorating 2D Bi4O5Br2 with 0D N-CQDs and comprehensively characterized. Accordingly, the successful establishment of 0D-2D N-CQDs-Bi4O5Br2 (CD-BOB) VDW heterojunction composites was confirmed. These VDW heterojunctions were employed for photocatalytic removal of NO at part per billion (ppb) level and showed enhanced photocatalytic outcome compared with BOB under visible light, mainly attributing to structural merits of 0D-2D morphologies integration, promoted transfer and separation of charge carriers, boosted adsorption and activation of O2 and H2O as well as reactants NO confirmed by density functional theory (DFT) calculations, and strengthened generation of abundant of reactive species. The best candidate 5 % CD-BOB achieved a maximum removal efficiency of 51 %, which was 1.89 times higher than that of BOB alone. Besides, the robust sample was able to efficiently inhibit the formation of toxic NO2. Moreover, a preliminary photocatalysis mechanism was eventually proposed based on the analytical and experimental results. This study offers valuable insights into constructing 0D-2D VDW heterojunction composites for efficiently photocatalytically removing NO under visible light illumination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen oxides (NOX), as one kind of the major environmental pollutants, can lead to acid rain, photochemical smog, greenhouse effect, and have significant impacts on human health even at low concentrations (approximately 21 ppb) (Xue et al., 2024). NOX is mainly composed of nitric oxide (NO) and nitrogen dioxide (NO2), with NO typically present at low concentrations within the ppb range (K Zhang et al., 2024b). Traditional treatment techniques are more suitable for NO removal at high concentrations and high flow (Lu et al., 2022). In contrast, semiconductor-based photocatalytic technology may combine light irradiation and semiconductors to produce highly oxidative and reactive substances for purifying environmental air pollutants. The characteristics of such a method include the easy conversion of light into chemical energy, operation under mild conditions, and avoidance of secondary pollution, ensuring a green and sustainable manner for effectively treating low-concentration NOX (Liu et al., 2024).

Bismuth oxyhalides (BiOX, where X = Cl, Br, I) have garnered attention from researchers due to their unique [X-Bi-O-Bi-X] structures that consist of interleaved stacked halogen ions and [Bi2O2]2+ layers (Nie et al., 2025). The resultant built-in electric field in these compounds benefits the separation of charge carriers and further photocatalytic performance (X Li et al., 2024b). Besides, Bi4O5Br2 is a typical two-dimensional (2D) semiconductor with a planar structure that offers abundant active sites and short carrier migration distances. However, the single-phased Bi4O5Br2 suffers from limited visible-light absorption, the severe recombination of produced charge carriers, and the weak adsorption capacity for oxygen and water molecules, resulting in the unsatisfactory performance of NO removal (Lian et al., 2024). Various modification strategies such as heterojunction construction (Zhu et al., 2019), doping (Zhang et al., 2020), noble metal deposition (Zhu et al., 2020), and carbon materials decoration (Zang et al., 2023) can commendably tackle these challenges. Among these modification strategies, the integration of different components into hetero-structured composites via physical or chemical interactions, such as covalent bonds, van der Waals forces, ionic bonds, and Coulombic forces, may significantly improve light response and interface electronic transmission (Zhou et al., 2022). However, conventional covalently-bonded semiconductors include lattice mismatch, interfacial defects, and deteriorated carrier recombination (Xu et al., 2021). In contrast, van der Waals (VDW) heterojunctions largely avoid issues related to lattice mismatch and generation of unsaturated dangling bonds during fabrication processes because their interfaces are established by the relatively weak van der Waals forces, rather than strong chemical bonds (Zhou et al., 2022). The structural flexibility enables the combination of components with different lattice constants, reducing stress and defects caused by lattice mismatch. Consequently, it enhances both the structural stability and catalytic behavior of target composites, thus providing potential possibilities for combining differential 2D materials. The carbonized polymer dots/potassium-doped carbon nitride VDW heterojunctions without strict lattice matching have been fabricated for enhancing photocatalytic CO2 reduction (Dong et al., 2024). The BiO2-x/Bi3NbO7 VDW heterojunctions have significantly enhanced photocatalytic O2 activation through efficient charge separation and transfer (Xu et al., 2022). The intimate contact in VDW heterojunctions optimizes the electronic structure, improving the interfacial separation and interlayer migration of charge carriers (Peng et al., 2022). 0D-2D VDW heterojunctions gather both the structural merits of large specific surface area and strong light absorption of 0D materials and the effective migration and segregation of charge carriers of 2D materials, largely benefiting the photocatalytic performance (Zhang et al., 2021). Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-modified ZnIn2S4/BiOCl VDW heterojunctions showed reinforced photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics under visible and near-infrared light (Jiang et al., 2019). In addition, N-doped CQDs-BiOIxBr1-x VDW heterojunctions could strengthen photocatalytic degradation efficiencies because of microstructural merits (Gao et al., 2021).

Carbon materials have found versatile applications in the field of photocatalysis, thanks to their abundant sources, low cost, and environmental friendliness (Bai et al., 2024). CQDs are a kind of novel 0D carbon materials with sizes smaller than 10 nm, large surface area, and short electron transfer path, which provide abundant reactive sites to enhance catalytic outcomes (Mondal et al., 2022). The incorporation of nitrogen atoms into CQDs (N-CQDs) may increase photoelectron transfer efficiency, tune work function, and induce charge delocalization (Wei et al., 2023). Additionally, N-CQDs can establish π-conjugated heterojunctions with semiconductors, thereby enhancing light harvesting capabilities and expediting charge carriers transfer (Wang et al., 2024; Yu et al., 2020). For example, N-CQDs decorated PrFeO3/palygorskite composites can induce improved NOX conversion and N2 selectivity during the visible-light-driven photo-SCR processes (Li et al., 2018). In addition, the electronic structure and interfacial charge migration could be well modulated in N-CQDs-modified TiO2 composites, accordingly enhancing NOX adsorption and photocatalytic oxidation under ultraviolet and visible light (Martins et al., 2016). As a result, the rational establishment of 0D-2D composites ensures not only the stable distribution of 0D N-CQDs onto 2D semiconductors but also the trimmed electronic structures that adsorb reactant molecules and promote charge carriers’ transfer. Thus, the 2D Bi4O5Br2 nanosheets were elaborately selected to construct 0D-2D N-CQDs-Bi4O5Br2 VDW heterojunctions that were subjected to structural analyses and photocatalytic NO removal evaluation, which had never been reported ever.

In this investigation, various 0D-2D N-CQDs-Bi4O5Br2 VDW heterojunction composites were fabricated through a facile in-situ method and fully characterized by a collection of analytical techniques. DFT calculations were conducted to illustrate the regulation of charge carriers at the interface and the adsorption and activation of O2 and H2O as well as reactants NO, which might be favorable for the photocatalytic NO removal together with structural merits of 0D-2D morphologies integration, and strengthened generation of abundant of reactive species. The VDW heterojunction composites showed enhanced photocatalytic performance of NO removal and avoided the generation of toxic NO2. In addition, the robust nature of these heterojunction composites was demonstrated by successive recycling experiments and structural stability. Eventually, a possible photocatalysis mechanism was proposed according to analytical and experimental results.

The detailed chemicals and reagents utilized were furnished in the Supplementary Materials to ensure brevity. N-CQDs were synthesized via a reported facile hydrothermal method (He et al., 2024) with slight modifications, denoted as CD. Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7 ·H2O, 1.26 g, 0.006 mol) and ethylenediamine (EDA, 1.08 g, 0.02 mol) were mixed in ultrapure water (30 mL), stirred, and sonicated for 30 min until fully dissolved. The solution was then placed in a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the brown-yellow liquid was placed in a dialysis bag and dialyzed for 48 h. The solution was then evaporated to 100 mL in an oven and stored in the dark. The pure Bi4O5Br2 abbreviated as BOB was synthesized via a simple water-bath procedure, following the procedure reported (Wu et al., 2018). The 0D-2D N-CQDs-Bi4O5Br2 composites were synthesized using an in-situ method, as depicted in Fig. S1. During the synthesis of pure Bi4O5Br2, N-CQDs with volume ratios of 1 %, 3 %, 5 %, and 7 % relative to the volume of the alkaline solution were separately added. As-achieved composites were named 0D-2D X CD-BOB, where X represented the volume ratios as mentioned. For conciseness, details on apparatus and equipment utilized for structural characterizations and density-functional theory (DFT) calculations were provided in Supplementary Materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic NO removal and reactive species capture
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic NO removal process under visible light as shown in Fig. S2 was identical to the previous study (Chang et al., 2023a), with specific details provided in the Supplementary Materials. The composite 5 % CD-BOB with the best catalytic performance was chosen for reactive species quenching measurements. During the photocatalytic procedure, potassium iodide (KI, 0.1 g), tert-butyl alcohol (TBA, 0.1 mM), p-benzoquinone (PBQ, 0.1 g), or β-carotene (0.1 g) was added to capture reactive specific such as holes (h+), hydroxyl radicals (∙OH), superoxide radicals (∙O2 −), or singlet oxygen (1O2), respectively. Reusability experiments were carried out in the presence of the composite 5 % CD-BOB, as described in the Supplementary Materials. The concentrations of nitrate (NO3 −) and nitrite (NO2 −) ions were determined on a UV–Visible spectrophotometer and the detailed procedures were provided in the Supplementary Materials. The standard curves of absorbance versus residual concentrations for nitrate and nitrite ions were respectively displayed in Fig. S3(a) and S3(b).

The crystal structure, composition, and purity of pure BOB and CD-BOB series were analyzed on an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) and are shown in Fig. 1 (a). The characteristic diffraction peaks of BOB align with those of the monoclinic phase Bi4O5Br2 (JCPDS No. 37–0699) (Zheng et al., 2017), with peaks at 2θ = 24.29°, 29.54°, 31.80°, 43.61°, and 45.49° corresponding to the (112), (11-3), (020), (32-3), and (422) crystal planes, respectively. It is noteworthy that the diffraction peaks of the CD-BOB series are quite similar to those of BOB, which suggests that the disordered carbon atoms of N-CQDs did not affect the crystal structure of BOB when it was attached to its surface (Naghash-Hamed et al., 2023). This can be attributed to the small particle size, high dispersibility, and low dosage of the dialyzed N-CQDs. The absence of additional peaks in composites indicates sufficient purity without the formation of other substances. Raman spectra of BOB and composite 5 % CD-BOB were further recorded and are shown in Fig. 1(b). Some prominent characteristic peaks at 76.3, 102.3, and 162.4 cm−1 are observed in both samples and respectively correspond to A1g external, A1g internal, and E1g internal Bi-Br stretching modes of BOB (Chang et al., 2021a; Zang et al., 2023), confirming the good conversation of the original structure of NOB without disturbance. However, some weak peaks in BOB are unable to be found in 5 % CD-BOB, possibly owing to the dispersion and accumulation of N-CQDs on the surface. In addition, the typical signals of D and G bands attributed to carbon materials are undetectable in composite 5 % CD-BOB, which is possibly relevant to the low dosage of dialyzed N-CQDs (Okla et al., 2023). This result agrees well with the XRD findings.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of BOB and composite 5 % CD-BOB were measured for analyzing microstructures and morphologies. The lamellar structures of BOB can be seen in the SEM image of Fig. 2 (a), the typical characteristic of 2D materials. These lamellae with a thickness of about 43 nm are densely packed to form hierarchical flower-like clusters. Similar microstructures and morphologies are found in the SEM image of composite 5 % CD-BOB in Fig. 2(b), revealing the ignorable disturbance of incorporated N-CQDs. The TEM images of BOB and 5 % CD-BOB are respectively depicted in Fig. 2(c) and (d). Both samples show similar lamellar morphologies in TEM images, which are consistent with the SEM images. In addition, obvious N-CQD slices within layers are unable to be seen in composite 5 % CD-BOB in Fig. 2(d) and instead, observable with a diameter of around 5 nm in Fig. S4 as white elliptic lines marked. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was also analyzed for BOB and 5 % CD-BOB. Clear lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.281 nm can be surveyed in the HETEM image of BOB in Fig. 2(e), corresponding to (020) crystal planes of Bi4O5Br2 (JCPDS No. 37–0699) (Wang et al., 2021). In Fig. 2(f), lattice fringes with the lattice spacing of 0.295 nm in 5 % CD-BOB are relevant to (113) crystal planes of Bi4O5Br2 (Shi et al., 2023). Additionally, several small dark regions about 5 nm in size, marked by white arrows, appear in Fig. 2(f), indicating the successful incorporation of 0D N-CQDs onto the surface of 2D BOB and in-situ formation of VDW heterojunctions.

The EDS spectrum of composite 5 % CD-BOB is presented in Fig. S5(a). The elemental scans for Bi, Br, O, C, and N are depicted in Fig. S5(b)–(f), respectively. The surface of 5 % CD-BOB is rich in elements Bi and Br with relatively lower levels of N, O, and C, while C and N are uniformly distributed. This indicates the presence of N-CQDs on the BOB surface, providing another evidence for the formation of 0D-2D VDW heterojunctions.

The chemical composition and elemental valence states of BOB and 5 % CD-BOB were determined via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS spectra for pure BOB and 5 % CD-BOB are presented in Fig. 3 (a), displaying peaks that correspond to Bi, O, Br, and C elements. Notably, no significant N signal is observed in 5 % CD-BOB, likely due to the low content and even distribution. The carbon signal in 5 % CD-BOB is notably stronger than in pure BOB, indicating the incorporation of N-CQDs. High-resolution spectra for C 1s, N 1s, Bi 4f, Br 3d, and O 1s are presented in Fig. 3(b)–(f). No additional peaks are observed except for the occasional carbon involved during analysis in Fig. 3(b). However, the peak area of C1s in 5 % CD-BOB is increased by 14.04 % over that in BOB, confirming the presence of C species from N-CQDs (T Li et al., 2024a). As shown in Fig. 3(c) for 5 % CD-BOB, both weak peaks of N 1s at 402.75 and 406.55 eV correspond to N-H and N-O bonds in N-CQDs with the low content (Li et al., 2019; Otgonbayar and Oh, 2023). In Fig. 3(d), distinct peaks at 158.71 and 164.01 eV relate to the Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 orbitals of Bi3+ in BOB (Chang et al., 2021b). The Br 3d spectrum in Fig. 3(e) exhibits peaks at 68.11 and 69.11 eV, attributed to the Br 3d3/2 and Br 3d5/2 orbitals of Br ions in BOB (Li et al., 2020). Notably, the binding energies of Bi and Br elements in 5 % CD-BOB shift towards higher fields compared to pure BOB, indicating a strong interaction between N-CQDs and BOB mediated by VDW forces and alteration of electronic structure in VDW heterojunctions (Jiang et al., 2019). For the O 1s spectrum in Fig. 3(f), the peaks at 529.45, 530.71, and 532.51 eV are individually associated with the lattice oxygen in the Bi-O bonds, surface adsorbed –OH, and other adsorbed components (Liu et al., 2022; G Zhang et al., 2024a). Moreover, in 5 % CD-BOB, the peak area ratio of -OH and H2O is nearly 20.65 % compared to that of 16.29 % in BOB, indicating the strong adsorption capacity over oxygen-containing species in the composite system. Significantly, the extra peak with a binding energy of around 282 eV is unobservable in the composite 5 % CD-BOB, excluding the possibility of generating Bi-C bonds (Zhou et al., 2024). This indicates that the physical adsorption occurs between BOB and N-CQDs. Additionally, DFT calculations reveal that the shortest distance from atoms C and N in N-CQDs to the BOB surface are respectively 2.96 and 3.30 Å, both of which are quite smaller than the van der Waals radii of Bi + C (3.77 Å) and Bi + N (3.62 Å). These results theoretically verify the successful formation of CD-BOB VDW heterojunctions within the range of VDW forces.

The optical response and band structures of the BOB and CD-BOB series were analyzed via UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS). As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the light absorption range of yellowish BOB spans from 200 to 500 nm, with an absorption edge at approximately 461 nm. Interestingly, a slight redshift in the absorption edge of the CD-BOB series with the increase of N-CQDs content is observed in the localized enlarged area, indicating the faint enhancement of the light-absorption caused by the formation of CD-BOB VDW heterojunctions (Jing et al., 2024). Besides, the tiny alteration of light harvesting can also be reflected by the almost same color of the CD-BOB series. Therefore, the absorption variation of visible light impossibly contributes to the amelioration of photocatalytic performance. The band gap energy (E
g ) of a semiconductor can be calculated using the formula αhν = A(hν-E
g ) n/2 (Huang et al., 2023), where α, hν, and A represent the absorption coefficient, photon energy, and a constant, respectively. The value of n depends on the type of electronic transition in semiconductors, with values of 1 and 4 respectively for direct and indirect transition. A value close to E
g is selected to plot a curve of ln(αhν) versus ln(hν-E
g ), and the value n is approximatively determined by calculating slopes of the linear regions near band edges (Chang et al., 2025). According to Fig. S6, the slope of BOB is 2.0473 (>1), and the value of n is thus adopted as 4, which is consistent with the previous report (Ye et al., 2016). E
g values of 2.27 and 2.26 eV for BOB and 5 % CD-BOB are depicted in Fig. S7, confirming the minimal effect of incorporated N-CQDs over the variation of E
g values in CD-BOB composites. The electronic structures of BOB and 5 % CD-BOB are derived from the crystal planes (202) and (113) in HRTEM images of Fig. 2(e) and (f), with the side view models presented in Fig. 4(b). Significantly, the blue-green electron transfer is found at the interface between N-CQDs and BOB. However, the evidence of chemical bonding between BOB and CD is absent, directly demonstrating the formation of VDW heterojunctions (Lee et al., 2023). The partial density of states (PDOS) for BOB and 5 % CD-BOB are shown in Fig. 4(c), indicating a slightly reduced bandgap width for 5 % CD-BOB compared to BOB. The positions of the conduction and valence bands are occupied by N-CQDs, indicating the successful construction of the CD-BOB composite system, where N-CQDs play a crucial role in promoting charge carrier transfer.

The band structures of BOB and 5 % CD-BOB were explored by associating the bandgap energies with electrochemical analyses to determine the flat band potential (V
fb ). The Mott-Schottky (M-S) plots for BOB and 5 % CD-BOB are respectively shown in Fig. 4(d) and (e). The positive slopes of M-S curves for BOB and 5 % CD-BOB realize the n-type semiconductor of both samples (Sun et al., 2018). Moreover, the same value of −0.4 V relative to the saturated calomel electrode is achieved for both BOB and 5 % CD-BOB. Based on the discrepancy between the saturated calomel electrode and the standard hydrogen electrode (NHE), the E
fb values of BOB and 5 % CD-BOB are about −0.16 V relative to NHE. For n-type semiconductors, the potential of the conduction band bottom (E
CB ) is typically 0.1–0.3 V higher than the E
fb value (Kumar et al., 2019) and an average value of 0.2 V is thus selected. Consequently, the E
CB values obtained for BOB and 5 % CD-BOB are −0.36 V, and corresponding potentials of valence band top (E
VB ) for BOB and 5 % CD-BOB can be respectively computed as 1.91 and 1.9 V using the formula E
CB = E
VB - E
g .

ce:section.ce:section-title: The efficiency of NO removal under visible light
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the visible light irradiation, NO removal experiments were conducted by using as-achieved catalysts in a continuous flow system. Each evaluation was conducted at least three times to obtain an average value with error bars to ensure accuracy and reliability. As shown in Fig. 5 (a) and S8, the blank experiment in the absence of any catalysts induces negligible NO removal, ruling out the possibility of NO oxidation under the tested condition and allowing us to assess the actual efficiency. The photocatalytic NO removal efficiency of VDW heterojunction composites increases under the same condition with the augment of CQDs content. Among all samples, the composite 5 % CD-BOB shows the best removal efficiency around 51 % after 5 min, which is 1.89 times higher than that of BOB (27 %). Additionally, the NO removal efficiency for composites 1 % CD-BOB, 3 % CD-BOB, and 7 % CD-BOB are around 39 %, 43 %, and 41 % under identical conditions, respectively. Furthermore, the apparent reaction performance constant (K
app ) can be utilized to evaluate the reaction efficiency of the CD-BOB series during the initial 5 min of the photocatalytic reaction, employing the kinetic formula ln(C
0 /C) = K
app ·t (Chang et al., 2025). From Fig. 5(b), it is evident that 5 % CD-BOB possesses the highest K
app value 19.25 × 10−2 min−1, approximately 2.14 times that of BOB. These results indicate that the 0D-2D VDW heterojunctions enhance the separation efficiency of charge carriers, thereby improving photocatalytic activity. NO elimination and relevant reaction conditions of some recent studies are collected in Table S1 for a comprehensive comparison, revealing the comparable and superior efficiency of our photocatalytic system.

The photocatalytic oxidation of NO mainly provides gaseous NO2 and NO3 − (NO2 −) anions, with a general reaction pathway as NO—NO2 ---NO2-- NO3 −. The NO2 generation by BOB and CD-BOB series during the photocatalytic processes ought to be considered since it is more toxic than the initial substrate NO (X Li et al., 2024b). Although 5 % CD-BOB insignificantly inhibits the generation of NO2 it is essentially almost the same as that of BOB in Fig. S8. Typically, the comprehensive removal efficiency of NOx is generally assessed by the combination of NO removal and NO2 production through the DeNO
X index (Nie et al., 2024). For the sake of brevity, the exact calculations are described in the Supplementary Materials. As depicted in Fig. 5(c), the composite 5 % CD-BOB gains the largest DeNO
X index among all samples, further illustrating the superior photocatalytic capability of 0D-2D VDW heterojunctions. Moreover, the generation of other species such as NO2 − and NO3 − has been a research focus during the photocatalytic removal of NO since the formed NO2 −/NO3 − ions are able to fertile farmlands and promote carbon cycling (Huang et al., 2023). The removal efficiencies of NO and NOX, as well as the selectivity for NO2 and NO2 −/NO3 − by BOB and CD-BOB series, are summarized in Fig. 5(d). Under continuous visible irradiation for 30 min, 5 % CD-BOB exhibits a NOX removal efficiency 1.97 times higher than that of BOB, with the lowest selectivity towards NO2. Moreover, 5 % CD-BOB displays approximately 1.67 times higher selectivity towards NO2 −/NO3 − compared to BOB. Overall, 5 % CD-BOB demonstrates an extraordinary selectivity towards NO2 −/NO3 − species, thus effectively preventing the formation of toxic NO2. To verify the above results, the concentrations of NO2 −/NO3 − on the surface of BOB and CD-BOB series were measured after the photocatalytic experiments with the specific experimental procedures described in detail in the Supplementary Materials. As shown in Fig. 5(e), more than twice the concentration of NO2 −/NO3 − can be found on the surface of composite 5 % CD-BOB compared to that on BOB after 30 min of light exposure. This observation is consistent well with the DeNO
X index and obtained selectivity. As a result, it indicates that the composite 5 % CD-BOB possesses satisfactory photocatalytic performance, effectively converting NO directly into nitrate species and avoiding the generation of toxic NO2, which is mainly attributed to the creation of 0D-2D VDW heterojunctions with adjustable interfacial charge carriers. To further identify the formation of NO2 −/NO3 − species, the N 1s XPS spectra of 5 % CD-BOB after subjecting to visible light for 200 min is recorded in Fig. 5(f). Clearly, the N 1s XPS spectra of 5 % CD-BOB after soaking in a NO2 − and NO3 − solution show both peaks at 402.98 and 407.18 eV, respectively corresponding to NO2 − and NO3 − species. After photocatalytic NO removal for 200 min, a characteristic peak at 406.68 eV is observed on the surface of 5 % CD-BOB, indicating the real generation of NO3 − ions (Chang et al., 2021a). However, the absence of another signal corresponding to NO2 − ions may be attributed to the relatively low content of NO2 − ions as shown in Fig. 5(e), and the ease of oxidation in the air (Wolff et al., 1998) during sending for analyses.

The segregation efficiency of photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+) can be analyzed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Chen et al., 2025). The fitting results of EIS demonstrate relatively small errors in Fig. 5(g) and relevant parameters are collected in Table S2. The composite 5 % CD-BOB has the smallest arc radius among all tested samples, exhibiting the lowest resistance to charge transfer during photocatalytic reactions, which can be verified by the smallest charge transfer resistance (Rct) in Table S2. Therefore, the surface charge transfer of 5 % CD-BOB occurs at the fastest rate. Experimental results demonstrate that the 0D-2D VDW heterostructure facilitates interfacial charge transfer in 5 % CD-BOB, effectively enhancing the photocatalytic activity of the composites. The electrochemical Bode plots of BOB and 5 % CD-BOB are shown in Fig. 5(h), where the Bode peak for 5 % CD-BOB shifts to a lower frequency compared to BOB. According to the formula τe = 1/(πf max) (Liu et al., 2020), where τe represents the carrier lifetime, and f max is the maximum frequency. As seen, f max is inversely proportional to the carrier lifetime, indicating that the carriers' lifetime of 5 % CD-BOB is much longer than that of BOB. Electrochemical analyses confirm the effective suppression of photo-generated carriers’ recombination in 0D-2D VDW heterojunction, thus facilitating the generation of more reactive species for photocatalytic NO removal. As a result, the close contact between 0D N-CQDs and 2D BOB through VDW heterojunctions effectively inhibits charge carrier recombination, thereby enhancing photocatalytic performance.

The recyclability and microstructural stability of composite 5 % CD-BOB were assessed through duplicated and long-term photocatalytic experiments. As shown in Fig. 5(i), the photocatalytic NO removal efficiency and NO2 generation can be maintained well even after four cycles, revealing the satisfactory recyclability of composite 5 % CD-BOB. In addition, the slight reduction of NO removal efficiency and increase of NO2 generation caused by 5 % CD-BOB occur after the reaction duration time is extended to 120 min in Fig. 5(j), mainly attributing to the coverage of active sites on catalysts surface by oxidative species accumulated. Expectfully, after simply rinsing with water, the photocatalytic NO removal efficiency of 5 % CD-BOB can be partially restored. The XRD patterns and XPS spectra of 5 % CD-BOB before and after the photocatalytic reaction are depicted in Fig. 5(k) and (l). There are no significant changes in spectra before and after the reaction, indicating the composition stability and structural robustness of the 0D-2D VDW heterojunctions and potential applicability for photocatalytic removal of NO at ppb level.

DFT simulations were utilized to estimate the adsorption and activation of NO, O2, and H2O molecules on both models BOB and CD-BOB, as respectively shown in Fig. 6 (a) and (b). The chemical bonding between BOB and NO or O2 molecules is undiscoverable, revealing the presence of physical adsorption. Instead, a weak chemical bonding between BOB and H2O molecules is found, causing a faint activation of H2O molecules because of merely a slight charge transfer. Nevertheless, the creation of VDW heterojunctions at the interface in the CD-BOB system promotes substantial charge transfer, thus favoring the absorption and further activation of NO, O2, and H2O molecules. Accordingly, the Gibbs free energy change of adsorption (ΔG
ads ) of O2 and H2O on the surface of BOB and CD-BOB was calculated and is shown in Fig. 6(a). The values ΔG
ads for O2 and H2O on CD-BOB (−0.59, −0.23 eV) are lower than those on BOB (0.31, 0.45 eV), demonstrating that O2 and H2O molecules can be effectively attracted and activated on CD-BOB other than BOB, which can also be reflected from the variation of bond lengths and bond angles in Table S3. For example, the smaller band length of Bi-O and the large band length of O-O in CD-BOB than in BOB, revealing the efficient adsorption of O2 on CD-BOB surface and further activation it by extension of O-O bonds. Besides, both smaller bond angles of Bi-O-O in CD-BOB than in BOB imply the activation of O2 through the strong interaction of Bi atom and both adjacent O atoms.

Potential possibilities of the adsorption and activation of NO as well as the formation of NO2 − and NO3 − species could be estimated by DFT calculations through computing the Gibbs free energy change. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the ΔG
ads value of NO molecules on CD-BOB is −0.33 eV which is significantly lower than that of 0.33 eV on BOB, meaning the thermodynamically spontaneous process of NO absorption on CD-BOB. The smaller bond length of Bi-N and larger bond length of N-O suggest the easy adsorption and activation of NO molecules in CD-BOB in Table S3. Overall, the presence of VDW heterojunctions facilitates the accumulation and activation of small molecules O2, H2O, and NO on the CD-BOB surface, thereby favoring the further photocatalytic NO removal reactions. Furthermore, the Gibbs free energy change of formation (ΔG
f ) of NO to NO2 − on CD-BOB is around −2.29 eV that is significantly lower than that of −0.31 eV on BOB, demonstrating the thermodynamically spontaneous processes of adsorbed NO to NO2 −. Besides, the value ΔG
f of further conversion of NO2 − to toxic NO2 or NO3 − on BOB is respectively around −0.87 or −0.49 eV, while the value on CD-BOB is respectively around −0.87 or −2.29 eV, demonstrating the thermodynamically spontaneous processes of NO2 − to toxic NO2 on BOB and instead NO2 − to NO3 − on CD-BOB. Thus, these results discover that the creation of VDW heterojunctions facilitates not only the adsorption and activation of reactants and oxidants but also the conversion of NO to NO3 −, which is consistent with experimental results shown in Fig. 5(d) and (e).

To gain insight into the contributions of specific radicals to NO removal under visible light on BOB and CD-BOB series, entrapping experiments were carried out by inducing various radical scavengers during photocatalytic NO removal reactions. In Fig. 7 (a) and (b), the introduction of TBA as a radical scavenger leads to a slight decrease in the photocatalytic activity of both BOB and 5 % CD-BOB compared to the blank experiment. Notably, the reduction of photocatalytic removal for 5 % CD-BOB is less pronounced than that of pure BOB, suggesting that ∙OH radicals may be the non-dominating active species. Upon the addition of KI or PBQ, the photocatalytic performance of BOB and 5 % CD-BOB is almost completely suppressed, highlighting the crucial roles of h+ and ∙O2 − radicals during photocatalytic processes. Moreover, the involvement of β-carotene results in a severe inhibition of photocatalytic outcome for both BOB and 5 % CD-BOB, suggesting the important role of 1O2 radicals. The generation of ∙OH radicals on the surface of BOB and 5 % CD-BOB after the visible light irradiation can be assessed in a basic solution of phthalic acid through photoluminescence (PL) spectra. As shown in Fig. S9(a), both samples induce quite weak fluorescence signals at 425 nm, implying the impossibility of ∙OH radicals generation, which is in good accordance with the aforementioned entrapping experimental results.

The production of reactive radicals such as ∙O2 −, 1O2, and ∙OH reactive species in both systems BOB and 5 % CD-BOB was detected by ESR measurements (Gao et al., 2025). Signals of DMPO-∙OH adducts are unable to be observable in the dark or under visible light by neither BOB nor 5 % CD-BOB in Fig. S9(b), excluding the possibility of radicals ∙OH generation, which agrees well with PL spectra and entrapping experiments. Signals DMPO-∙O2 − adducts in methanol and TEMP-1O2 adducts in water in dark and under visible light are respectively depicted in Fig. 7(c) and (d). In both catalytic systems ESR signals cannot be found in the dark, but indeed appear upon visible light irradiation. In addition, VDW heterojunction 5 % CD-BOB causes more intensive ESR signals of 1O2 and ∙O2 − radicals than BOB under identical conditions, revealing the boosted generation of reactive radicals by efficient adsorption and activation of molecular O2 and strengthened separation of charge carriers across the interface.

Based on entrapping experimental results and band structural analyses, a preliminary photocatalysis mechanism is proposed to describe the transfer of charge carriers and the formation of reactive species on the CD-BOB composites. 0D N-CQDs are grafted and anchored onto the surface of 2D Bi4O5Br2 nanosheets to form 0D-2D VDW heterojunctions, like eggs scattered on grassland as shown in Fig. 8 . The creation of 0D-2D VDW heterojunctions has little impact on the optical properties and band structures, as shown in Fig. 4. Instead, it tunes interfacial property and regulates produced charge carriers, enhancing adsorption and activation of O2 and H2O in Figs. 3f and 6, as well as reactants NO in Fig. 6, further promoting the formation of abundant reactive species such as h+, ∙O2 −, and 1O2 in Fig. 7. Under visible light, 5 % CD-BOB is able to excite and inject e− onto the conduction band (CB) and remains h
+ on the valence band (VB). Some e− react with adsorbed molecular O2 to form radicals ∙O2 − because of the more negative E
CB (−0.36 V) than the redox potential (O2/∙O2 −, −0.046 V) (Chang et al., 2021b), as proven in Fig. 7(b) and (c). Other e− may transfer to electron acceptor N-CQDs (Zhong et al., 2021) and further convert to radicals ∙O2 −, thus promoting the separation of charge carriers. Besides, h
+ remained on VB is impossible to produce radicals ∙OH because of the more negative E
VB (1.9 V) than the redox potential (OH−/∙OH +1.99 V and H2O/∙OH 2.38 V) (Zhang et al., 2020), which is confirmed by Fig. 7(b) and S8. In contrast, h
+ remaining on VB may react with radicals ∙O2 − to generate reactive 1O2 (Chang et al., 2023b). The achieved reactive species such as h
+ , ∙O2 −, and 1O2 may react with adsorbed NO molecules to produce NO2 −/NO3 − species because of potentials NO2 − (NO2 −/NO, 0.99 V) and NO3 − (NO3 −/NO, 0.94 V) (Chang et al., 2021b), as shown in DFT calculations in Fig. 6(b). As a result, because of the structural merits of 0D-2D morphologies integration, promoted transfer and separation of charge carriers, boosted adsorption and activation of O2 and H2O as well as reactants NO, and strengthened generation of abundant reactive species, these 0D-2D VDW heterojunctions CD-BOB show enhanced photocatalytic performance over NO removal with avoidance of producing more toxic NO2. Together with its robust nature, these Bi4O5Br2-based 0D-2D VDW heterojunction composites may be practically utilized in fields of photocatalytic removal of NO at ppb levels under visible light illumination.

In this study, a series of 0D-2D CD-BOB VDW heterojunctions were successfully created by modifying 2D Bi4O5Br2 with 0D N-CQDs via an in-situ method and systematically analyzed. These as-synthesized VDW heterojunctions were utilized for photocatalytic removal of NO at ppb level and exhibited strengthened photocatalytic behavior compared with BOB in visible light, preliminarily owning to structural merits of 0D-2D morphologies harmony, increased transfer and separation of charge carriers, promoted adsorption and activation of O2, H2O, and NO, and boosted generation of abundant of reactive species. Among all tested samples, composite 5 % CD-BOB achieved the highest removal efficiency of 51 % which was 1.89 times higher than that of BOB alone. In addition, the sample could efficiently avoid the formation of NO2. Moreover, the composite 5 % CD-BOB was quite robust enough to initiate five successive recycling experiments without an obvious decrease in photocatalytic capability. Finally, a possible photocatalysis mechanism was proposed to describe the transfer path of charge carriers and subsequent oxidation reactions. This investigation provided meaningful insights into establishing 0D-2D VDW heterojunction composites for efficient removal of NO upon the photocatalytic technique.

Fei Chang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Jiayi Li: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Wenhao Bai: Investigation. Yibo Lei: Investigation. Deng-guo Liu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Yuan Kong: Writing – review & editing, Software.
